fr:mainmatter.html:p: "FDE" stands for "first-degree E". E is a logic like FDE, but E uses a very different implication connective. The logic E was first developed to use this implication connective to give some understanding of entailment; that's why it's called E.

E is a member of a family of logics known as relevant logics (in Australia) or relevance logics (in the US). Relevant logics form a very important aspect of Australian research in logic---indeed, they're the reason that I first came to Australia. They're a bit too complex for these notes to spend much time on them, but if you want to look into them, you'll find a fascinating literature.

fr:title.text: Another example logic, presented with a matrix

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Another example logic, presented with a matrix

fr:mainmatter.html:p: To give you some practice with this idea, here's another example logic. This one I'll present directly via a matrix. The structure we'll use for this one is the Dunn-Belnap structure, and the designated values are \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}. There; that's enough to fully specify a logic. This one is one we'll call FDE.

"FDE" stands for "first-degree E". E is a logic like FDE, but E uses a very different implication connective. The logic E was first developed to use this implication connective to give some understanding of entailment; that's why it's called E.

E is a member of a family of logics known as relevant logics (in Australia) or relevance logics (in the US). Relevant logics form a very important aspect of Australian research in logic---indeed, they're the reason that I first came to Australia. They're a bit too complex for these notes to spend much time on them, but if you want to look into them, you'll find a fascinating literature.
Like all the logics we've seen so far, FDE holds the argument p \land q \;/\; p to be valid, and p \lor q \;/\; p to be invalid.
Take a moment and see if you can find an FDE counterexample to this second argument.

There are a number of different ways to reach an FDE counterexample to the second argument. If you have a valuation v where v(p) is either \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} and where v(q) is either \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}, then you have a counterexample to this argument; if your valuation isn't like this, then you don't have one.

Let's think it through: to be a counterexample to p \lor q \;/\; p, a valuation would need to take p \lor q to one of our designated values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}, and take p to some undesignated value. Since the undesignated values in this matrix are \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}, we know that for v to be a counterexample we need v(p) to be either \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}. What, then, about v(q)? Well, we also need v(p \lor q) to be either \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}. By checking how the Dunn-Belnap structure treats disjunction, you can see that, with v(p) being either \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}, setting v(q) to either \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} will work, and nothing else will.

What about the arguments that distinguished K3 and LP from classical logic? Here, too, FDE is different from everything we've seen so far. Like K3, and unlike classical logic or LP, FDE holds \;/\; p \lor \neg p to be invalid; any valuation v where v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} provides a counterexample. And like LP, but unlike classical logic or K3, FDE holds p, \neg p \;/\; q to be invalid; any valuation v where v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} and v(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} provides a counterexample. For the same reason, FDE agrees with LP in holding disjunctive syllogism to be invalid, unlike classical logic or K3.
